subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD diagnosis with comorbid anxiety
stem:
A 32-year-old accountant attends your general practice clinic with a 2-year history of worsening concentration, difficulty completing tasks, and missed deadlines. He says 'I have to write down every step or I lose track, my desk looks like an explosion, and I keep losing my keys'. He has also had persistent worry about work performance, initial insomnia, and muscle tension for the past 18 months. School reports from age 8 describe a 'bright but disorganised' boy whose teachers commented on inattention and inability to sit still.

He drinks 4 standard drinks a week and does not use other substances. There is no mood instability, no psychotic symptoms, no recent head injury, and no thyroid history. Thyroid function is normal on bloods this morning. He scored 22 on the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (cut-off 14 for probable diagnosis) and 16 on the GAD-7 (cut-off 15 for moderate to severe anxiety). His worry is unrelated to specific objects and is generalised across multiple domains over more than 6 months.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Restless during interview, fidgety, frequent loss of train of thought; mental state otherwise unremarkable, no psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), normal FBC and UEC; ASRS-v1.1 score 22; GAD-7 score 16

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence a long-acting stimulant such as lisdexamfetamine for adult ADHD pending psychiatry confirmation later
options.rationale: Stimulant prescribing for adult ADHD in Australia is restricted to authorised prescribers (psychiatrists, paediatricians, and some neurologists depending on state) and requires diagnostic confirmation by a specialist before initiation. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Position Statement; AMH)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily for generalised anxiety and review in 6 weeks before any ADHD work-up
options.rationale: Treating only the anxiety risks missing or worsening the ADHD; comorbid presentations are best assessed by a specialist with shared diagnostic formulation. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Position Statement)

options.text: Refer to a psychiatrist for adult ADHD assessment, treat anxiety with first-line measures in parallel, and discuss driving safety
options.rationale: Adult ADHD diagnosis requires specialist confirmation in Australia via collateral history (school reports), structured assessment, and exclusion of differentials. Comorbid generalised anxiety disorder is common (over 50% of adult ADHD) and is treated in parallel with first-line CBT or SSRI when symptoms are moderate to severe. Stimulant safety includes a discussion of driving impairment during titration. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Position Statement; ICGP Adult ADHD)

options.text: Order an electroencephalogram and brain magnetic resonance imaging before any further behavioural assessment occurs
options.rationale: EEG and MRI are not part of the routine ADHD work-up and are reserved for atypical features (head injury, focal deficits, paroxysmal symptoms). (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Position Statement)

options.text: Provide cognitive behavioural therapy alone for both inattention and anxiety and review at 12 weeks for response
options.rationale: CBT alone for ADHD has modest benefit and is most effective as an adjunct to pharmacotherapy in moderate to severe ADHD; sole CBT delays effective treatment. (Source: RANZCP Adult ADHD Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Adult ADHD presents with persistent inattention, organisational difficulty, restlessness, impulsivity, and emotional dysregulation continuing from childhood. Diagnosis requires evidence of symptoms before age 12, impairment in two or more domains, and exclusion of differentials. Collateral history (school reports, family) supports diagnosis. Comorbid anxiety, depression, substance use disorders, and learning disorders are common and treated in parallel. Stimulants (lisdexamfetamine, methylphenidate) are first-line pharmacotherapy in Australia and require authorised prescriber initiation. Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant option, particularly when stimulant adverse effects emerge or substance misuse risk is significant. Driving safety advice is part of the discussion.
key_points: Specialist confirmation required for adult ADHD diagnosis in Australia
Comorbidity is the rule, not the exception
First-line: long-acting stimulant by authorised prescriber
Driving safety counselling during titration
explanation.pearls: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale Part A (ASRS-v1.1) of 4 or more positive items raises pre-test probability sufficiently to refer for diagnostic interview; the score itself is a screening tool, not diagnostic.

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P1 woman is brought by her partner to your emergency department on day 9 postpartum. He says 'she hasn't slept for three nights, she's saying the baby has been swapped at the hospital and the radio is sending her messages'. She has had no prior psychiatric history but her mother has bipolar I disorder. She gave birth to a healthy term boy by normal vaginal delivery; the postnatal period was uneventful initially.

On examination she is alert but tangential, with rapid pressured speech and labile affect. She describes the radio referring to her by name and intermittent visual perceptions of 'shadows changing shape'. There is no fever, no rash, and no neck stiffness. She is breastfeeding and her baby is in good health. There is no acute risk identified in the moment but she repeatedly asks 'are they going to take him away because he is the wrong baby?'. Bedside vitals are unremarkable; haemoglobin 124 g/L, white cell count 9.4 x10^9/L, CRP 4 mg/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, urinalysis clear.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Tangential, pressured speech, labile affect, delusional content (baby identity, radio referential), visual misperceptions; no neck stiffness, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Hb 124 g/L (115-150), WCC 9.4 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 4 mg/L (under 5), TSH 1.8 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), urinalysis clear

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg orally daily for severe postnatal depression and arrange community midwife visit tomorrow
options.rationale: This is postpartum psychosis (psychotic features, sleeplessness, mood lability, abrupt onset within the first weeks postpartum), not postnatal depression. SSRI alone does not treat psychosis. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Provide reassurance, schedule a 1-week postnatal follow-up review, and continue breastfeeding without pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Outpatient management of postpartum psychosis is unsafe; suicide and infanticide risks are elevated and admission to a mother-baby unit is the AU standard. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Refer urgently to a community mental health team for case management without inpatient admission at this stage
options.rationale: Community management is reserved for mild postnatal mental illness with intact reality testing and adequate supports; psychotic features mandate inpatient mother-baby unit care. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Admit to a mother-baby unit, commence olanzapine, and consider lithium with a breastfeeding shared decision
options.rationale: Postpartum psychosis is a psychiatric emergency with a significant suicide and infanticide risk; admission to a mother-baby unit allows joint care while ensuring infant safety. An antipsychotic (olanzapine or risperidone) is first-line; lithium has strong evidence for prevention of recurrence and acute treatment, with shared breastfeeding decisions about infant lithium monitoring. ECT is considered for severe or refractory cases or when rapid response is needed. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement; COPE Perinatal Mental Health Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Arrange outpatient cognitive behavioural therapy for postnatal adjustment and review in 4 weeks at her next visit
options.rationale: CBT does not treat acute psychosis and outpatient settings cannot guarantee infant safety; this is an emergency. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

explanation.summary: Postpartum psychosis affects 1-2 per 1000 births, typically within the first 2 weeks postpartum, with abrupt onset of psychotic features, mood symptoms (often manic or mixed), sleeplessness, and confusion. Past or family history of bipolar disorder or previous postpartum psychosis is the strongest risk factor. Differentials include delirium, autoimmune encephalitis, severe postnatal depression with psychotic features, and substance-induced psychosis. Management is admission to a mother-baby unit when available, antipsychotic therapy, lithium for acute and prophylactic indication, and ECT for severe or refractory cases. Suicide and infanticide risks are elevated and the infant is never left alone with the unwell parent during the acute phase.
key_points: 1-2 per 1000 births; first 2 weeks
Family or personal history of bipolar disorder is the major risk
Admit to mother-baby unit (when available)
Risk of suicide and infanticide remains until resolution
explanation.pearls: Women with a history of postpartum psychosis or bipolar I have approximately 50% risk of relapse in a subsequent pregnancy; prophylactic lithium at delivery is the AU recommended preventive intervention in shared planning.

sources.label: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective vs bipolar with psychotic features
stem:
A 31-year-old man with a 6-year mental health history attends your community psychiatry clinic for diagnostic clarification. His parents say 'when he's high he doesn't sleep, he talks fast, he buys things he can't afford, and he hears voices then'. He has had four hospital admissions: two for manic episodes with psychotic features (delusions of being a religious prophet, persecutory voices) and two for major depressive episodes with mood-congruent psychosis. Between episodes he has been functioning at work part-time.

Review of his records confirms that during 2 distinct periods, each lasting 4 to 6 weeks between admissions, he experienced delusions of telepathic communication and persecutory auditory hallucinations without concurrent prominent mood symptoms (his mood was 'flat but not depressed' and energy was preserved). His insight is generally good now. There is no substance misuse. He is currently on lithium 900 mg at night, lamotrigine 200 mg daily, and risperidone 4 mg at night, and is in partial remission. He asks 'what is my diagnosis?'.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 124/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic at presentation, no current psychotic features, MMSE normal, no extrapyramidal signs
data_table.Investigations: Lithium 0.7 mmol/L (0.6-1.2), TSH 2.0 mIU/L (0.5-4.0), eGFR 84 mL/min/1.73 m squared
data_table.Medications: Lithium 900 mg PO nocte; lamotrigine 200 mg PO daily; risperidone 4 mg PO nocte

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features confined entirely to mood episodes and no inter-episode psychosis
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychotic symptoms occurring only during mood episodes; the documented inter-episode psychosis disqualifies this label. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with mood episodes secondary to neuroleptic adverse effects and demoralisation
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires 6 months of disturbance with at least 1 month of active-phase positive symptoms; the prominent and recurrent mood episodes with mood-congruent psychosis are too prominent for schizophrenia. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type given prominent mood episodes and inter-episode primary psychotic symptoms
options.rationale: This option correctly identifies the schizoaffective construct but uses imprecise terminology; the DSM-5-TR requires the major mood episodes to be present for the majority of the total active illness duration and at least 2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms - the question's diagnostic phrasing differs. The intended best option pairs the bipolar type label with the specific 2-week inter-episode psychosis criterion. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder mixed type with comorbid bipolar I disorder and substance use diagnostic complication
options.rationale: Delusional disorder requires non-bizarre delusions for at least 1 month without hallucinations or other psychotic features prominent enough to meet schizophrenia criteria; this patient has prominent hallucinations and prior psychotic episodes that exceed that. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type with documented psychosis lasting at least 2 weeks without prominent mood
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type requires (1) an uninterrupted period during which there is a major mood episode (depressive or manic) concurrent with criterion A of schizophrenia, (2) delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks in the absence of a major mood episode during the lifetime of the illness, (3) mood symptoms present for the majority of the active and residual phases. This patient meets all three. The bipolar subtype is assigned because manic episodes have occurred. (Source: RANZCP Mood Disorders CPG; DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is differentiated from bipolar I with psychotic features and from schizophrenia by the temporal relationship between mood and psychotic symptoms. DSM-5-TR requires: (1) major mood episode (depressive or manic) concurrent with criterion A of schizophrenia, (2) delusions or hallucinations for at least 2 weeks without a major mood episode at any time in the lifetime of the illness, (3) mood symptoms present for the majority of the total active and residual periods. Subtypes are bipolar type and depressive type. Management combines antipsychotic with mood stabiliser (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine) and structured psychosocial intervention. Longitudinal records and collateral history are essential to differentiate from bipolar I with psychotic features.
key_points: At least 2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT major mood episode (lifetime) - the discriminator
Concurrent major mood episode plus criterion A of schizophrenia
Mood symptoms present for the majority of active and residual phases
Subtype by lifetime presence or absence of manic episodes
explanation.pearls: In schizoaffective disorder, mood symptoms occupy the majority of the total active and residual illness; if they occupy a small fraction with chronic psychosis dominating, the diagnosis is schizophrenia with mood episodes.

stem:
A 68-year-old man is brought to your emergency department by ambulance with 6 hours of confusion and rigors. His wife says 'he was shivering, then he went pale and floppy on the couch and stopped making sense'. He has a background of type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 140 micromol/L). He has not had recent surgery. He has had increasing urinary frequency and dysuria over the past 3 days.

On examination he is drowsy and disoriented to time and place, with mottled peripheries, a capillary refill of 4 seconds, and warm core. There is no neck stiffness. Abdomen is soft with left renal angle tenderness. Bedside lactate is 4.6 mmol/L (under 2.0). VBG shows pH 7.30 (7.35-7.45), HCO3 18 mmol/L (22-26). FBC shows WCC 18.4 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0), neutrophils 16.2 (2.0-7.5), platelets 96 x10^9/L (150-450). Creatinine 220 micromol/L (45-90), CRP 240 mg/L (under 5). Urinalysis: nitrites positive, leukocyte 3+. He has received 250 mL of normal saline en route.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 122 regular, BP 84/52 mmHg (MAP 63 mmHg), RR 28, SpO2 94% on room air, temperature 38.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, disoriented x2, mottled peripheries, CRT 4 seconds, left renal angle tenderness, no rash, no neck stiffness
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.6 mmol/L (under 2.0); VBG pH 7.30 bicarbonate 18 mmol/L; WCC 18.4 with neutrophilia, platelets 96 x10^9/L; creatinine 220 micromol/L (baseline 140); CRP 240 mg/L; urine nitrites positive leukocyte 3+
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD; gliclazide MR 60 mg daily; perindopril 4 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Send blood cultures, give IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg single dose plus IV ampicillin, and 1 L crystalloid fluid bolus
options.rationale: Crystalloid resuscitation for sepsis-induced hypotension or lactate above 4 mmol/L targets 30 mL/kg within 3 hours, not a single 1 L bolus; gentamicin dose is reasonable but needs review in CKD and is paired with a broader-spectrum agent for urosepsis with shock. (Source: ANZICS Surviving Sepsis Adaptation; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Give intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as first-line therapy for the septic shock physiology immediately
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is added when shock persists despite adequate fluid resuscitation and vasopressor therapy; it is not the first-line step. (Source: ANZICS Surviving Sepsis Adaptation)

options.text: Take blood cultures, give 30 mL/kg crystalloid over the first 3 hours, IV piperacillin-tazobactam, lactate trend, and call ICU
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis adapted hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, take blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay, administer broad-spectrum empirical antibiotics within 1 hour (piperacillin-tazobactam covers Gram-negative urosepsis), give 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4 mmol/L, repeat lactate, start vasopressors to maintain MAP at least 65 mmHg if hypotension persists after fluids. Early ICU communication is part of escalation. (Source: ANZICS Surviving Sepsis Adaptation; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Hold antibiotics until urine culture returns a positive organism and titrate fluid by hourly central venous pressure
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay in septic shock increases mortality by approximately 7% per hour; empirical broad-spectrum therapy is given before culture results. CVP-guided resuscitation has been superseded by dynamic measures. (Source: ANZICS Surviving Sepsis Adaptation)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline immediately before any fluid resuscitation to restore mean arterial pressure to 65 mmHg
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is initiated when fluid resuscitation has been inadequate to restore MAP to 65 mmHg, not before initial fluid loading; in undifferentiated shock, fluid resuscitation precedes vasopressor in the first hour. (Source: ANZICS Surviving Sepsis Adaptation)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign 1-hour bundle (adapted in Australia by ANZICS) includes measuring lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4 mmol/L, and vasopressor (noradrenaline) to maintain MAP at least 65 mmHg if fluid-refractory. Repeat lactate guides response. Empirical antibiotic choice is by source. Urinary tract sepsis is treated with piperacillin-tazobactam or third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime), with gentamicin added when severity warrants and renal function permits. Source control (drainage, removal of foreign material) is essential.
key_points: Lactate, cultures, antibiotics, 30 mL/kg fluid, vasopressor if refractory - the hour-1 elements
Antibiotic delay raises mortality - empirical cover starts immediately
Source control completes the bundle
Noradrenaline first-line vasopressor; vasopressin second
explanation.pearls: Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor in septic shock targeting MAP 65 mmHg; vasopressin 0.03 IU/min is added to reduce noradrenaline requirements when high doses are needed.

stem:
A 52-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to your emergency department with 36 hours of severe left thigh pain and erythema, having scraped his shin on a metal gate at work 2 days ago. He says 'the pain is way worse than the redness looks, paracetamol does nothing, and now my whole leg feels numb in patches'. He has had a fever overnight and one episode of vomiting this morning. His diabetes is managed with metformin and an SGLT2 inhibitor.

On examination he is pale and unwell. The left thigh is erythematous, swollen, exquisitely tender, with patchy dusky areas and a single haemorrhagic blister on the antero-medial surface. Crepitus is palpable along the medial thigh. Sensation is reduced over the medial aspect of the thigh. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. WCC 21 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 280 mg/L (under 5), sodium 132 mmol/L (135-145), creatinine 156 micromol/L (45-90), glucose 16.4 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L, haemoglobin 122 g/L. LRINEC score is 9.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 124 regular, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 26, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 38.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Erythematous swollen left thigh with patchy duskiness, haemorrhagic blister antero-medially, palpable crepitus medially, reduced sensation over medial thigh
data_table.Investigations: WCC 21 x10^9/L, CRP 280 mg/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, creatinine 156 micromol/L, glucose 16.4 mmol/L, lactate 3.8 mmol/L, Hb 122 g/L; LRINEC 9
data_table.Medications: Metformin 1 g BD; dapagliflozin 10 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Computed tomography of the thigh with intravenous contrast to delineate the extent of fascial gas before referral
options.rationale: Imaging is supportive but should not delay surgical referral and exploration when clinical features are characteristic; the operating theatre is the diagnostic and therapeutic destination. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin and gentamicin and admit under general medicine for serial limb examinations
options.rationale: Empirical cover for necrotising fasciitis requires broad spectrum including anaerobic and protein synthesis inhibitor for toxin suppression; surgical debridement is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical referral for theatre debridement, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics including clindamycin, and fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Necrotising soft tissue infection (LRINEC over 6, pain out of proportion, dusky skin, haemorrhagic blisters, crepitus, systemic toxicity) is a surgical emergency. Empirical broad-spectrum cover (meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin (for streptococcal and clostridial toxin suppression) and fluid resuscitation accompany urgent surgical debridement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; ANZBA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection)

options.text: Outline the erythema and discharge home on oral cephalexin with review by general practitioner in 24 hours
options.rationale: This is a surgical emergency; outpatient management has a high mortality. Cephalexin does not cover the spectrum needed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Apply topical mupirocin and adjacent silver sulfadiazine dressings with daily wound clinic review for 1 week
options.rationale: Topical agents have no role in deep soft tissue infection requiring debridement; this is a surgical emergency. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a deep soft tissue infection along the fascial planes with rapid progression and high mortality. Hallmarks: pain out of proportion to examination, dusky discoloration, haemorrhagic blisters, crepitus, systemic toxicity, and a tense oedematous limb with reduced sensation over the affected area. LRINEC score 6 or higher raises suspicion (CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine, glucose) but does not exclude the diagnosis at lower scores. Management is urgent surgical debridement, broad-spectrum empirical antibiotics (meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam) plus clindamycin for toxin suppression, and aggressive fluid resuscitation. Source control is essential and re-look debridement is the rule.
key_points: Pain out of proportion plus dusky skin plus crepitus is a surgical emergency
LRINEC over 6 raises suspicion; under 6 does not exclude
Empirical cover: meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam PLUS clindamycin
Repeat debridement is the rule, not the exception
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin is added to standard broad-spectrum cover because it suppresses streptococcal toxin production and remains effective in the stationary growth phase where beta-lactams are inoculum-limited.
